evidence_excerpt: security must be enforced deterministically at the execution boundary, not probabilistically at the prompt layer. This architectural approach follows the same principle that led to the adoption of system call filtering, sandboxing, and capability-based security in modern operating systems.

evidence_excerpt: Extensible agents also collapse two supply chains into one execution loop. They combine an untrusted code supply chain, such as skills, plugins, and connectors, with an untrusted instruction and data supply chain, such as web content, email, documents, and tool outputs.

notable_evidence_or_examples: CVE-2026-25253 (OpenClaw vulnerability); OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (Agent Goal Hijack, Tool Misuse, Supply Chain Vulnerabilities).
evidence_excerpt: prompt-level guardrails and in-context alignment are fundamentally insufficient when an agent possesses shell, file, and network access. True security requires an "OS-Level Wall" combined with runtime hardening.

notable_evidence_or_examples: Hypothetical autonomous scheduling agent merging tenant and landlord inspection appointments, creating statutory access violation, data protection breach, and professional liability simultaneously. Concrete case illustrating objective hierarchy inversion.
evidence_excerpt: I call this objective hierarchy inversion. It happens when an agent correctly optimizes for a procedural sub-objective while silently violating the higher-order constraint that sub-objective was built to serve. No external actor is involved. The failure emerges from the architecture itself.

notable_evidence_or_examples: Model Context Protocol (MCP) cited as enabling tool-using agents. Three-phase threat evolution described (2023-2024 isolated model risks; 2025-present tool-using agents; emerging multi-agent systems). 30+ years enterprise infosec experience noted.
evidence_excerpt: The pace of these changes suggests that static security guidance will be insufficient. NIST should consider building revision mechanisms into any published framework, with a review cycle no longer than 12 months.

specific_recommendations: 1) NIST guidance should address the calibration challenge for human approval gates, providing a risk-based framework for which action categories require human review. 2) NIST should establish standard oversight categories to improve underwritability of agentic AI. 3) Prioritize indirect prompt injection in guidance. 4) Recommend least-privilege, sandboxing, action logging, and explicit action boundaries as essential controls.
notable_evidence_or_examples: MIT AI Risk Repository, OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications, NIST AI Risk Management Framework, NIST research on agent hijacking evaluations cited. Armilla's own underwriting/evaluation framework referenced.
evidence_excerpt: Deployers lack a shared vocabulary for characterizing human oversight configurations in ways that are both technically precise and actuarially useful. NIST guidance that establishes standard oversight categories—even at a high level—would materially improve the underwritability of agentic AI systems

specific_recommendations: NIST should define minimum functional criteria for exportable verification evidence; develop adversarial conformance tests; create procurement language extending existing frameworks; treat deterministic binding for streaming/multipart/multimodal outputs as a research priority; establish stable conformance model for evidence-based admissibility at the point of effect.
notable_evidence_or_examples: References work on indirect prompt injection; cites traditional TOCTOU failures as analogy; references existing NIST frameworks as foundational but incomplete for agent action boundaries. No specific datasets or external studies cited.
evidence_excerpt: the decisive control question is whether the point of effect requires admissibility evidence that verifies under policy. For each action class, the relevant relying party should be able to answer three questions: What exact artifact or transaction is this decision about?

specific_recommendations: 1. Consequence-tiered action thresholds: human review based on impact/reversibility. 2. Autonomous action logging for monitoring, validation, post-event reconstruction. 3. Rollback/remediation prerequisites before deploying agents with irreversible actions. 4. Security testing should include out-of-distribution action testing and full action trajectory evaluation.

evidence_excerpt: Many failures in agent systems are sequential rather than atomic. The core testing question is not only whether an agent produces a wrong answer, but whether a series of individually plausible actions compounds into an unsafe outcome.

specific_recommendations: NIST should pursue research and standards work around structured agent execution models, capability-based tool access, and composable action graphs to improve AI agent security.

evidence_excerpt: Instead of an agent directly executing actions, the agent generates a typed computation graph describing the intended sequence of operations. That program can then be validated, inspected, simulated, or modified before anything actually runs.

specific_recommendations: 1) Standardized agent identity/configuration attestation (analogous to TLS certs). 2) Declarative, auditable agent specification formats. 3) Agent-specific extensions to SP 800-63 and SP 800-207. 4) Tool-level access control standards. 5) NIST adopt OWASP agentic taxonomy as reference for guidelines.

evidence_excerpt: Agent systems connected to tool servers (such as MCP servers providing access to databases, APIs, or file systems) are typically granted access to the full tool surface of each connected server. In our experience, this violates the principle of least privilege in practice.

evidence_excerpt: NCCoE Zero Trust Architecture project (NIST SP 1800-35, June 2025) documented this fragmentation: 4 years, 24 technology providers, 19 different implementations — all proprietary. Enterprises face $500K-$2M custom integration costs per deployment. ZTNA adoption has stalled.

specific_recommendations: NIST should adopt DNS-anchored agent identity (ANS model) using ACME, DANE, Certificate Transparency, and SCITT. Standardize versioned Identity Certificates for agents. Require transparency logs for agent lifecycle events. Define maximum revocation propagation delays by risk level. Adopt five-dimensional Trust Vector scoring framework.
notable_evidence_or_examples: GoDaddy's Agent Name Service (ANS) live since November 2025; IETF draft-narajala-ans-00; RFC 8555 (ACME), RFC 6962 (CT), RFC 6698 (DANE), IETF SCITT, RFC 8785 (JCS), W3C Verifiable Credentials 2.0; open spec at github.com/godaddy/ans-registry.
evidence_excerpt: Any software endpoint that answers a protocol handshake correctly can claim to be any agent. No one can independently verify the claim. No public record captures what the agent declared at registration.

specific_recommendations: Adopt a four-dimensional reliability evaluation framework (consistency, robustness, predictability, safety) grounded in safety-critical engineering. Use multi-run/multi-condition evaluation protocols, perturbation testing, confidence elicitation, and harm-severity-weighted metrics rather than single-run accuracy scores.
notable_evidence_or_examples: Empirical study of 14 frontier AI models (OpenAI, Google, Anthropic), 500+ benchmark runs, 12 reliability metrics. NYC government chatbot (2024); Moffatt v. Air Canada (2024 BCCRT 149). Standards: DO-178C, ARP4754B, IEEE 603, ISO/PAS 21448, IEC 61508.
evidence_excerpt:
Our research
provides both a measurement framework grounded in safety-critical engineering and
systematic empirical evidence characterizing this risk.

evidence_excerpt: This asymmetry, where developers disclose far more about what agents can do than about how safe they are, constitutes a form of safety washing that undermines informed risk assessment.

Partner with private entities via NCCoE to pilot architectures in DoD contexts. 6. Accelerate publication to preserve U.S. leadership.

specific_recommendations: NIST should establish a standard for composition-specific behavioral evaluation of AI agent skills. Evaluation should cover per-skill security property testing across model compositions, not just static analysis. Continuous re-evaluation required when model versions change.
notable_evidence_or_examples: Empirical study: 10 AI agent skills, 186 security properties, 2,282 tests, ~6,800 evaluations; 9/10 skills degraded at least one security property; 1password skill rated 1/10 risk by industry scanner produced -25.6pp regression on exfiltration prevention; LLM static risk ratings inversely correlated
evidence_excerpt: We evaluated 10 non-malicious, community-approved AI agent skills across 186 security properties and found that 9 of 10 degrade at least one security behavior when loaded into a base model — even when existing static scanners rate them as safe.

specific_recommendations: NIST should: (1) distinguish hardware-bound vs. software-based passkey implementations and recognize hardware-based phishing-resistant credentials as higher-assurance; (2) develop standards for hardware-attested human authorization of AI agent actions (cryptographic authorization primitive) binding action, agent, factual context, and time; (3) require authorization records to detect context drift before execution.
notable_evidence_or_examples: FSSCC/U.S. Treasury AIEOG (Feb 2026): credential phishing rose 703% in H2 2024; LLMs reduce phishing costs 95%. NCCoE concept paper on Software and AI Agent Identity (Feb 2026). EU PSD2 secure display model. SolarWinds breach cited. Better Identity Coalition policy stance referenced.
evidence_excerpt: proving that a human being was explicitly in the authorization path of a specific consequential action at the moment of authorization. We call this the agent authorization problem, and it cannot be solved by authentication alone.

specific_recommendations: Implement layered controls: ephemeral identity, least privilege scope binding, observability, human oversight gates, fail-safe containment, delegation depth limits. Use three-horizon assessment: pre-deployment threat modeling, deployment validation, post-deployment behavioral monitoring. Constrain environments via enforced tool manifests, network segmentation, data perimeter controls, execution sandboxing.

evidence_excerpt: human oversight is foundational to safe and secure agentic AI deployment. Autonomous capability without explicit accountability, scope control, and oversight introduces unacceptable risk in environments where system integrity, safety, and resilience are paramount.

specific_recommendations: NIST/CAISI should develop new security abstractions for the computational layer introduced by AI agents; reexamine and adapt least-privilege and fine-grained access control principles to dynamic agent behavior; craft novel defenses tailored to agentic browsing environments.

Tiered human approval gating for high-consequence actions. 5. Suggested metrics: unauthorized tool-call block rate, blast radius, mean time to revoke, privilege escalation rate.

evidence_excerpt: A PEP enforcing task-scoped permissions rejects those calls regardless of how they were generated. The warrant constrains what the agent can do, independent of what it is instructed to do.

specific_recommendations: 1) Treat shared-kernel containers as insufficient for multi-tenant remote agent deployments. 2) Require hardware-level isolation (microVMs) as baseline for remote agent sandboxing in multi-tenant/sensitive-data environments. 3) Require 3-tier defense-in-depth: per-session VM, hypervisor monitor, and VPC boundary. 4) Provide guidance on agent initialization logging, authorization controls, and verified base images.

evidence_excerpt: We strongly recommend that NIST make full VM-based isolation the required sandboxing mechanism for cloud-based (remote) AI agent systems, especially for multi-tenant deployments and deployments processing sensitive data.

NIST/CAISI to drive standards for safety cases, capability evaluations, and reporting frameworks targeting scaled frontier developers.
